title: Metalwork & Smithing
description:
Room 07 covers metalworking traditions across Appalachian blacksmithing and cross-cultural metalwork from the Inuinnait copper working tradition and Northwest Coast First Nations copper and silver work.

Appalachian blacksmithing was the technological backbone of pre-industrial mountain communities. The blacksmith made and repaired tools (axes, hoes, draw knives, wedges), forged hardware (nails, hinges, door hardware, chains), shod horses (farriery), and fabricated custom implements for neighbors and community. No other craftsperson was more essential to a working Appalachian settlement. Appalachian smithing draws on Scots-Irish and German blacksmithing traditions brought to the central and southern Appalachian mountains from the 1700s onward — bringing European forge techniques and adapting them to locally available materials (charcoal from hardwood, iron from itinerant peddlers, later from local bloomeries).

The forge setup: a fire pot (firebox) fed by a bellows or electric blower, burning coal or charcoal to produce intense localized heat; an anvil (the working surface — horn, face, hardy hole, pritchel hole); hammers (cross-peen for drawing, rounding hammer for fullering, sledge for heavy work); tongs for gripping hot work; and hardy tools (chisels, punches, fullers) for cutting and shaping. The smith reads the metal temperature from heat color — a skill that takes practice but can be learned: black heat (cold to 400°F, looks cold but may not be), faint red (~900°F), cherry red (~1400°F, standard forging range), orange (~1600°F, upper forging range), yellow-white (~1900°F+, fire welding range). The piece is worked hot, returned to the fire when the heat fades, and brought back to temperature. A skilled smith manages multiple pieces in sequence, keeping the fire productive at all times.

Specific Appalachian metalwork traditions: nail-making (one of the earliest Appalachian home manufactures — wrought iron nails were valuable commodities before machine-cut nails arrived in the 1790s); knife-making and edged tool production (hunting knives, butchering knives, froe blades, draw knives — the Bowie knife heritage runs through Appalachian knife smithing); tool repair (reforging worn faces on hammers and hatchets, welding broken tool handles to heads, re-tempering springs); horseshoe fabrication and fitting (farriery was a major part of any active smith's work — horses were working animals, shoes wore out, and remote communities could not wait for a traveling farrier).

Heat treatment is a separate skill within smithing: hardening (heating steel to austenitizing temperature, then quenching rapidly to produce martensite — the hard, brittle phase) and tempering (reheating to a lower temperature to reduce brittleness and improve toughness). Getting the quench right determines whether a blade will hold an edge or shatter. The quench medium (water, brine, oil, commercial quench oil) affects how rapidly the steel cools — faster quench = harder steel with more risk of cracking.

Cross-reference: Copper Inuit (Inuinnait) copper working — the Inuinnait of the Coronation Gulf region (what is now western Nunavut) worked naturally occurring copper deposits using cold-hammering techniques without any forge or applied heat. Native copper nodules were found at surface exposures and on beaches. The Inuinnait hammered these nodules cold, exploiting the work-hardening property of copper to produce sharp edges on knives, ulus, and scrapers. This is a fundamentally different metalwork tradition from blacksmithing: no forge, no fire, no smelting — pure mechanical deformation. The Inuinnait are a distinct Inuit group; their name means 'real human beings' in Inuinnaqtun. See Room 14 (Arctic Living) for broader Inuit tool context.

First Nations copper and silver work (cross-reference): Northwest Coast nations, particularly the Haida and Coast Salish, have copper-working traditions involving both traditional copper wealth objects (shield-shaped objects known as 'coppers' used in potlatch exchange) and more recent silver engraving traditions. The Haida silver engraving tradition, developed in the 18th-19th centuries using European silver dollars, is one of the most sophisticated metalwork traditions in North America. Coppers in Northwest Coast tradition carry significant ceremonial and economic dimensions at Level 2-3 — the technical production of a copper is Level 1, but the ceremonial role of coppers in potlatch (including the practice of breaking a copper as the ultimate gift) belongs in Phase 37 rooms (Cedar Culture, Village World). Do not attempt to describe potlatch protocols here; cross-reference Phase 37 for full Northwest Coast cultural content. Nation attribution: Haida, Coast Salish.

modules.title: Forge Fundamentals: Setup, Fire Management, and Safety
modules.description: Forge construction and setup: fire pot selection (bottom-draft vs. side-blast), tuyere and ash dump, refractory lining, stand construction. Fuel types: coal (bituminous or metallurgical coke preferred; avoid anthracite for forge work) vs. charcoal (hardwood charcoal, burns cleaner, lower temperature ceiling). Bellows operation and electric blower use. Fire management: building and maintaining a fire ball in the center of the fire pot, clinker management (clinker is the fused ash residue that must be broken up and removed to maintain air flow), coal management (banking coal around the fire to pre-heat before it enters the fire ball). Heat management: regulating air blast to control fire temperature. Fire welding temperature: the highest working temperature in blacksmithing — metal surface appears wet and sparks fly. Quench tank: water or oil filled, within arm's reach of the anvil. Safety equipment: leather apron (protects from scale and sparks), appropriate footwear (leather, no synthetics), face shield or safety glasses, ear protection for extended hammer work.

modules.title: Hammer and Anvil: Fundamentals of Hand Forging
modules.description: Hammer types: cross-peen hammer (the standard blacksmith's hammer — flat face for planishing and general work, cross peen for drawing and spreading), rounding hammer (for fullering and rounding), sledge hammer (for heavy stock with a striker). Hammer weight selection for stock size. Grip and stroke technique: controlled strokes from the elbow, not the wrist; exhaustion is a major cause of missed blows and injury. Anvil anatomy: face (primary work surface, must be clean and hard), horn (for bending and round work), step (for cutting), hardy hole (for hardy tools), pritchel hole (for punching through). Hardy tools: hardy chisel (for cutting hot stock), punch (for hole-making), fuller (for drawing and creating shoulders). Basic operations: drawing out (lengthening and thinning stock by hammering along its length), upsetting (shortening and thickening by hammering on the end), bending (forming angles and curves over the horn or edge), punching (creating holes).

modules.title: Knife Making: Forging, Heat Treatment, and Finishing
modules.description: Stock selection: high-carbon steel (1084, 1095, O1, or salvaged material such as old files, leaf springs) vs. stainless (not recommended for forge beginners due to higher alloying requirements). Stock removal method (grinding from flat bar) vs. forging (shaping by hammer work at the forge). Blade geometry: blade profile (drop point, clip point, skinner), bevel geometry (full flat grind, hollow grind, Scandi grind, convex), edge angle selection for use case. Forging sequence: drawing the blade profile, forging the distal taper (blade thins toward tip), normalizing (stress-relief heat cycles). Heat treatment: austenitizing (heating to non-magnetic temperature, approximately 1475-1525°F for 1084 steel), quenching (oil quench for most high-carbon steels; brine or water for some carbon steels — consult steel data sheet), tempering (draw back at 375-450°F for 1-2 hours to reduce brittleness). Handle materials: wood (stabilized or sealed hardwood), bone, antler, Micarta. Finish: file and sand to final grind geometry, sharpen, fit handle.

modules.title: The Appalachian Smith: Community Role and Mountain Smith Tradition
modules.description: The community role of the blacksmith: in any pre-industrial Appalachian community, the smith was the most technologically central craftsperson. Unlike other trades, smithing could produce and repair almost any metal object — which meant the smith could keep a farm running when equipment failed, produce specialized tools no peddler carried, and fabricate hardware that would otherwise require a long journey to a distant market town. The mountain smith was also a problem-solver: people brought challenges, and the smith had to improvise solutions from available materials. Appalachian smithing tradition as documented in the Foxfire volumes: the Foxfire volumes (published from the 1970s onward) contain interviews with working Appalachian smiths documenting their tools, techniques, and practices. The forge as community space: the blacksmith shop was a warm, bright gathering place in cold seasons — a place for news, problem-solving, and community connection. The tradition of the traveling tinker and smith circuit-riding between remote communities. Contemporary Appalachian blacksmithing revival through craft schools (John C. Campbell Folk School, Penland School of Crafts) and the Artist Blacksmith's Association of North America (ABANA).

modules.title: Inuinnait Cold Copper Working: The Coronation Gulf Tradition
modules.description: The Inuinnait (Copper Inuit) of the Coronation Gulf region — what is now western Nunavut, in the traditional territory around Coppermine (Kugluktuk) — worked naturally occurring native copper using cold-hammering techniques without forge or applied heat. Native copper occurs as metallic nodules and masses at surface outcrops in the Coppermine River basin; the Inuinnait collected these nodules and worked them mechanically. Cold-hammering technique: the copper is struck with a hammerstone to shape it. Copper work-hardens as it is deformed — it becomes harder and more brittle. Annealing (heating briefly to relieve work hardening) was used by some groups, but the core technique is mechanical deformation without sustained high heat. Tools produced: knife blades, ulu blades, scrapers (for hide processing), needles, awls, and harpoon parts. The Inuinnait copper knife is a sophisticated tool — the cold-hammered copper holds a functional edge suitable for butchering and hide work. This tradition is distinct from blacksmithing (no fire), from smelting (no ore reduction), and from Northwest Coast copper-working (which uses commercially traded European sheet copper in later periods). Cross-reference: Room 14 (Arctic Living) for Inuit tool context and hide processing. Nation attribution: Inuinnait (Copper Inuit — Inuinnaqtun: Inuinnait means 'real human beings', the nation's self-name for this distinct Inuit group of the Coronation Gulf region).

modules.title: First Nations Copper and Silver Work: Northwest Coast Traditions
modules.description: Northwest Coast nations have metalwork traditions working both native copper and, in later periods, imported European copper and silver. Copper wealth objects: the shield-shaped object known as a 'copper' is one of the most significant material objects in Northwest Coast potlatch traditions. Coppers are large, T-shaped objects of beaten copper with a central ridge, engraved with clan crests. Each copper has a name and a history, and its value increases with each potlatch transaction. The construction technique (working copper sheet into the shield form and engraving the surface) is Level 1 technical knowledge; the ceremonial role of coppers in potlatch — their naming, gifting, breaking as the ultimate demonstration of wealth — is Level 2-3 and is addressed in Phase 37 rooms. Haida silver engraving: the Haida developed a distinctive engraving tradition working European silver coins and later silver sheet, producing jewelry, bracelets, and ceremonial objects engraved with the Northwest Coast formline design system. This tradition developed in the 18th and 19th centuries as European silver became available through trade. Coast Salish metalwork: Coast Salish peoples have traditions of both copper and silver work, often incorporating traditional geometric design systems. Nation attribution: Haida, Coast Salish. Note: this module provides a cross-reference to Phase 37 rooms (Cedar Culture, Village World) where full Northwest Coast cultural sovereignty framework applies.
